skill.name: pdf
skill.description: PDF processing toolkit for extracting text and tables, creating and modifying PDFs, splitting or merging documents, rendering pages, and filling PDF forms with helper scripts.
skill.summary: Process, analyze, generate, merge, split, render, and fill PDF documents.
skill.author: ZhanlinCui

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many external command, filesystem, network, weak-crypto, and C2 keyword matches. Manual review found legitimate PDF documentation and helper scripts, with no prompt injection, exfiltration, malware intent, or hidden network calls. The skill remains medium risk because it guides agents to run local scripts and PDF command-line tools that read and write user-selected files.

medium_findings.title: Local File Read and Write Workflows
medium_findings.description: The helper scripts read user-provided PDF or JSON paths and write PDFs, images, or JSON outputs. This is expected for PDF processing, but users should run it only on trusted local paths and review output destinations.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly open paths from command arguments and write local output files. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it is a real filesystem capability.

medium_findings.title: External PDF Tool Commands in Documentation
medium_findings.description: The instructions include shell commands for tools such as pdftotext, qpdf, pdftk, and pdfimages. These are local PDF utilities, not hidden execution paths, but they can modify or decrypt files when used by an agent.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and relevant to PDF manipulation. I found no evidence that the skill dynamically constructs shell commands or executes them without user intent.

low_findings.title: Documentation URLs Misclassified as Network Activity
low_findings.description: The hardcoded URLs are references in comments to PDF specifications, form behavior, or library issues. No HTTP client, fetch call, credential transfer, or outbound request was found in these locations.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines are comments only. There is no network library usage or runtime request tied to these URLs.

low_findings.title: High-Risk Keyword Matches Are False Positives
low_findings.description: Static matches for weak cryptography and C2 terminology come from PDF documentation terms, Markdown backticks, tests, and examples. No evidence found of malware control channels, credential theft, or weak cryptographic implementation.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found benign PDF processing context at representative static-hit locations. The high-risk labels do not align with the actual code behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Read and Write Workflows
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper scripts read user-provided PDF or JSON paths and write PDFs, images, or JSON outputs. This is expected for PDF processing, but users should run it only on trusted local paths and review output destinations.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The scripts directly open paths from command arguments and write local output files. The behavior is legitimate for this skill, but it is a real filesystem capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: External PDF Tool Commands in Documentation
dangerous_patterns.description: The instructions include shell commands for tools such as pdftotext, qpdf, pdftk, and pdfimages. These are local PDF utilities, not hidden execution paths, but they can modify or decrypt files when used by an agent.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command examples are explicit and relevant to PDF manipulation. I found no evidence that the skill dynamically constructs shell commands or executes them without user intent.

content.user_title: Process PDFs with Scripts and Examples
content.value_statement: PDF work often needs extraction, conversion, form filling, and document assembly across different tools. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical workflows and scripts for common PDF tasks.
actual_capabilities: Extract text and tables from PDFs using pypdf, pdfplumber, and related examples.
Create new PDFs with reportlab and JavaScript pdf-lib examples.
Merge, split, rotate, crop, encrypt, decrypt, and watermark PDF files.
Detect fillable form fields and prepare JSON field metadata for form filling.
Render PDF pages to images for visual inspection, OCR, and form placement.
Validate manual form bounding boxes before adding text annotations to PDFs.
limitations: It depends on local Python libraries and external PDF tools that may not be installed.
Scanned PDFs require OCR, and OCR accuracy depends on image quality.
Form filling may need manual validation when PDFs lack real form fields.
It does not provide built-in sandboxing for file paths or output locations.

use_cases.title: Prepare Completed Forms
use_cases.description: Identify PDF form fields, map values, validate placement, and generate a completed document.

use_cases.title: Extract Document Data
use_cases.description: Pull text, tables, page images, and coordinates from reports for review or downstream analysis.

use_cases.title: Assemble PDF Deliverables
use_cases.description: Merge, split, rotate, watermark, and create PDF files for client-ready document packages.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Basic PDF Text
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF skill to extract readable text from this PDF. Summarize each page and note pages with missing or unclear text.

prompt_templates.title: Fill a PDF Form
prompt_templates.prompt: Use the PDF form workflow to check whether this PDF has fillable fields. Then prepare the field mapping and create a filled output PDF.

prompt_templates.title: Convert and Inspect Pages
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert this PDF to page images, identify where each requested value should appear, and validate the bounding boxes before adding annotations.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Processing Pipeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a repeatable PDF pipeline that extracts tables, splits selected pages, adds a watermark, and writes clear output files with validation steps.

output_examples.input: A two-page application form and a list of values to enter.
output_examples.output: A completed PDF, a field mapping summary, and notes about any fields that required visual verification.

output_examples.input: A quarterly report PDF with tables and embedded figures.
output_examples.output: Extracted table summaries, page-by-page text notes, and a list of pages where image extraction may be useful.

output_examples.input: Several PDFs that need to become one client packet.
output_examples.output: A merged document plan with page order, rotation changes, watermark placement, and final output filename.

best_practices: Use a dedicated working folder so generated PDFs, images, and JSON files are easy to review.
Preview output PDFs after every merge, split, form fill, or annotation step.
For sensitive documents, avoid unnecessary OCR or conversion outputs and delete temporary images when finished.
anti_patterns: Do not run destructive qpdf or overwrite commands without confirming the exact input and output paths.
Do not trust automatically detected form positions without visual validation on non-fillable PDFs.
Do not process confidential PDFs in shared folders unless access controls are clear.

faq.question: Can this skill fill real PDF form fields?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes a workflow to detect fields, extract field IDs, validate values, and write a filled PDF.

faq.question: Can it fill PDFs without form fields?
faq.answer: Yes, but it uses visual bounding boxes and text annotations, so manual validation is important.

faq.question: Does it support scanned PDFs?
faq.answer: Yes. It describes converting pages to images and using OCR, but results depend on scan quality.

faq.question: Does it require external software?
faq.answer: Some workflows need Python packages or command-line tools such as qpdf, pdftotext, pdftk, or poppler utilities.

faq.question: Is it safe for confidential documents?
faq.answer: It works locally, but users should control file paths, temporary images, and access to generated outputs.

faq.question: Can it create PDFs from scratch?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes examples for creating PDFs with reportlab and pdf-lib.
